speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number three, September 5, 1995. And the survivor is Tema Lichtenstein. Tema, you were telling us about when you were interrogated by the Gestapo. What was happening to Mr. Novak and his family at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was in prison. When the-- his son saw the Germans coming to the village, he ran away. He was aware of who I was. And-- but they trusted him somehow. But he was told that the minute Germans come, he has to run, so they don't get a chance to talk to him. And his wife was away somewhere. And it was like in the morning-- was after breakfast. Whether she went to a neighbor or whatever-- I don't remember what. But she wasn't home-- just Novak and myself. And that's when they came in and arrested me. When they were interrogating me, they also-- I understand-- did it to him a couple of times, but, of course, separate. And he was also in the same-- same jail. But what was interesting is this Novak. Because his life was at stake too. If they would kill me, they would definitely kill him too. But on the way to the truck to take us to prison, he said to me, if they tell you that I told them already the truth, don't believe it. I'll never do it. And that helped me. That helped me an awful lot. And he said, even if they take you out to be killed, you must yell and scream to get the priest. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] But I want to also go back a little bit to the Novak family, that when I got there-- well, at first I was in hiding, and then he told them that I was a cousin. But those were people that only had a little store. They didn't have land. So they didn't have their own flour or anything. And the Germans closed also the schools for the Polish children. So I started to take groups of children-- different ages-- and teaching them. And we always had a little-- a dug-out hole, that if the Germans-- if we see the Germans are coming-- and the people know to come and warn us. The books would be thrown into that hole, because otherwise the same thing. They-- they killed for whatever reason. And so-- but I didn't want to be paid by the people. But I-- I used to get from them-- sometimes if they killed a-- a pig, they would make some sausage and give me. They would give me some flour. And that I gave to the family. So-- and-- and at least this much I can-- I can do to-- I could do at the time to help-- without-- well, of course, besides taking over all the chores in the house, you know, with laundry, and bringing water, and whatever had to be done. But they-- they really saved my life. Because I had somewhere-- when I escaped, I had somewhere to go to. And-- and even in the crucial moment when I was arrested, when this was the-- I thought this was the end already. I still had that in my mind that no, he did not tell them who I was. So I could-- I could still go on fighting.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Novaks survive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they did survive. But then even the young-- so after the war-- I don't remember details. But after the Germans-- we had Volksdeutsche, so-called Volksdeutsche-- which means the Germans that were born in Poland. And, of course, they were helping the Germans that came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they ran when the-- when the Russians came in, they ran. And a farm was left. And that's when Novak came and took it over-- after-- after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And God, I am thinking back. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [SIGHS] [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There were a few people in the-- no, one-- one other person in the village that knew who I was. And that was the one that I was a little bit afraid. But he proved himself. He didn't-- he didn't say anything. But when the Germans came and arrested me, I think the whole village was crying. You know, they-- and when I came back, there was-- there was a big party. But see, there was-- there were people in the cell with me that knew who I was-- in prison. And when they let me go, I was afraid to go back to the same place. I had to hide somewhere else. Because I thought if the Germans came-- the Novaks didn't go back to their place. Because they were afraid also for, you know, that the Germans may come. But they didn't have much time. Four days after I was liberated, the Russians came in. But the four days, I didn't stay there-- neither did the Novaks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you stay?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, Novak had a sister in a distant village, so we all went there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Why do you think Novak helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the first place, he was a nice man. And then-- I don't know. Maybe he felt that he-- oh, he said to me, as long as he was going to find a place-- [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Tema, before the power went off, you were telling us about why you think Mr. Novak helped you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the first place, he was a wonderful man. He was a very nice man. And then he knew about me, because I was going to be the person to come with that little boy to live in that village somewhere-- so maybe as a favor, maybe, to his good friends that made arrangements with him-- I don't know, maybe in their memory, or whatever.

speaker: interviewer
text: I can't-- I can't explain it. Because we didn't have anything. We couldn't pay him. You know? There was just no way. So he just had to be a good human being. What was his first name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Teofil.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: T-E-O-F-I-L.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you go after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: After liberation, I went to my hometown and to see who survived. And not trusting the Poles when I came back, I-- I saw a woman with a-- with a child in her arms. And I-- and I asked her, are they-- I'm just passing by this city. And somebody gave me a note-- if I find any Jewish people in the-- in this town, to give it to them. She said, oh yes. There is a-- there is a watchmaker, and he lives-- you know. She gave me the address. And I knew right away who it was. Well, he became my husband later. But I knew who he was right away because of the address where he lived. And he was a watchmaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: David Lichtenstein. And then I couldn't-- I didn't know what to do with myself. There were a few people that came back. I was-- I just didn't know what to do. I went back to the Novaks. And then I got-- I went to Starachowice, to this town. And people met me there. And they said, there is a postcard for you from somebody asking to-- if anybody knows what happened to the family from Plock, with the two daughters-- to please write on this address-- which was a Russian address. And I didn't know Russian.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I printed the address. And I answered that card. It was my sister who escaped to Russia. And-- and then when I wrote to her, she told me that they're coming back to Poland-- that I should be patient. What is your sister's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister is Eda. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And-- so-- and then I-- I stayed in Plock for a while. And that's when we-- but then the pogrom came in Kielce. So-- and it got to be-- it got to be very, very, very bad for us to stay. We just felt that we can't-- can't stay anymore. We can't trust the Poles.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went to Warsaw, where my sister lived. And from there, we went-- we went in the direction of Germany. And we were crossing borders. And we had already then a child-- four months old. When were you married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- [LAUGHS] --in '45. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was no big wedding. There was no big wedding at the time. [LAUGHS] There weren't any weddings. As a matter of fact, we had to get married again in Sweden to make it legal. After we went to Sweden, we had to make it legal. And we got married again.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you said you had a child.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's the name of your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harriet. Her name is now Heddy-- Heddy Lichtenstein. And her married name is Newman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was she born?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was born the 10th of March, '46. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say that you traveled out of Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, well, we were crossing borders, you know, in trucks and on-- we were mostly walking. And we had the buggy with us. So that was easy. The baby was in the buggy. And we got where we went-- we traveled through Austria and Germany. And we were in Czechoslovakia. Well, finally, we got to Germany. And we lived in Kaufbeuren for a while. And my-- my husband had family in-- in America. And he definitely wanted to go to America to be with his people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we went to Germany in order to wait there for our-- it's-- it's a-- well, it's a visa, really-- to come to America. So we spent 4 and a 1/2 years in-- in Sweden. You said you were in Germany waiting for a visa?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, yeah. Well, in Kaufbeuren. We lived in Kaufbeuren. And from there we went to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long were you in Sweden?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Sweden we were 4 and a 1/2 years. But by the way, I didn't-- I didn't tell you how my family met after the war. When I went to Warsaw, my sister that came back from Russia, was already in Warsaw. And then I heard that my mother came to Warsaw. We found on the list of survivors-- in Poland, in Warsaw-- we found the name of my father, that he survived Dachau. But my mother didn't know anything. So we were sure that she's not alive. But she was the one that came to Poland to see the children, whoever survived, to see whether anybody survived. And then she went back to-- to Germany. And she took my younger sister with her. I was already married at the time, with a baby. And we went to Sweden. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: So as-- as it turned out, my whole family really survived, which was unheard of. And I was teaching the-- the Polish children catechism. And I prepared them for their first communion while in the village, you know? And I did whatever-- whatever it-- [LAUGHS] was called for at the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: But without Teofil Novak, I would have never survived-- never. Because with going through the typhoid fever in camp, and my sister getting sick, and-- I would have never survived it. So I really owe it all to them. And it so happened that his son got killed in a car accident. He-- his wife went away somewhere. I think she had another man. And Novak died. So when I-- when I tried to get in touch with them, I heard from Novak's sister. And that's how I found out what happened to the Novak family.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you come to America?

speaker: interviewee
text: In-- in February '51.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: We moved to Chicago. We were two weeks only in New York, and then we went to Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of work did your husband do?

speaker: interviewee
text: What kind of what, please?

speaker: interviewer
text: Work did he do?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, he was a watchmaker/jeweler. So he found work in his trade. And Heddy was at the time five years old-- my daughter-- and she started school. And we stayed in Chicago. And then we opened our own business. And we were successful, I must say. And then we stayed in Chicago till we retired and came to Florida. And that's where I live now. And my husband died in November of '82.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Tell us about your grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my oldest is 25. He's in school still. He's planning to go to medical school, which we all hope he will. Davida is 11 years old, and she's very artistically inclined-- very sweet child. And Esther is the youngster, also a beautiful little girl-- wonderful. But my husband, of course, doesn't know that he had two more grandchildren. He died before she had the two children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you think that your experiences during the war affected the way you lived your life and raised your child?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have. It must have, because when I see now children mostly demanding things, and-- I never had it with my daughter. She always wanted to first know how was business today. And then she would ask for whatever she wanted to have. She knew that she could not ask for too much, that we're working hard. We're doing our best. And, of course, she knew quite a bit of what we went through. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Have your religious beliefs changed since before the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: I would say yes. I saw too much of things that I couldn't even bring out now, you know, things that were done to children. And it's almost impossible to put up old perspective and remember things, you know, the way they were happening. And so a lot of things were omitted. But after you go through things like that, it's very hard to-- to still believe. It-- it's easier, probably, if you believe, if you can believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there anything else you would like to tell us that you have omitted?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, unless you have certain questions for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you ever have dreams about your experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, that's another thing-- nightmares. I used to wake up and scream. It took my husband time to calm me down. He said, open your eyes. See where you are. Also, my worst dreams were that I had my daughter in camp with me. And I knew that if she cries out, she'll be killed. She'll be taken away. And I had to hide her-- all the time hide her and worry-- she shouldn't cry out. Those were the worst nightmares. There I was-- I was shaking for the longest time after waking up already. Oh, but the nightmares were almost constant. They're maybe not as often now, but I'm still many times in camp during the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] It's 50 years.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's 56 years since the war broke out. The 1st of September was 56 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: And 50 years since the end of the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what would be your message to your child and your grandchildren?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not to take anything for granted, to be aware of everything, to know what's going on-- because if it could happen in Germany, it can happen anywhere. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: To constantly be aware-- I have friends that say, well, it's time maybe to forget already. How long can this go on? And, of course, I don't talk to them about it at all. Because it's no use.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have to tell you-- I think, in Sweden, where a woman approached me-- she said, yeah, I understand you went through the Holocaust in Poland. And you must have gone through an awful lot. And before I had a chance to answer-- by the way, we had to speak German. I didn't speak any Swedish at the time. And she said, oh, we had it very hard here too. And she continued. Meat was rationed, and coffee was rationed. Well, I had-- I had nothing to say anymore. If those were the tough times, then I had nothing to add to it. But a lot of people-- and I don't blame them. Unless you were there, you can't even imagine. And words can never describe it. I can close my eyes and see it more than to bring it out in words. I really can't. I omitted. I can think of a lot of things that I omitted in this interview. But it-- it-- it's almost impossible. Oh, it's-- it used to-- there were times when I met together with my sister after the war. She wanted to know what went on during the war, because she was in Russia. And I remember I was lying in bed with her and telling her. That was so fresh after I was arrested by the Gestapo, and freed, and liberated.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was telling her. And she turned to me. She said, how can you imagine things like that? My own sister couldn't believe it. Tema, why do you think you survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know- mostly looks and luck-- that's all. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nothing else. Everything was-- worked against-- typhus in camp, and other things-- and escaping, where I was-- easily could have been killed right there on the spot when I escaped from camp. And why I survived? I don't know. I-- I really can't tell.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, well, this is-- those are my parents-- my mother, Frajda Najman and my father, Jacob Zvi. Oh, Najman was her maiden name-- Zylbersztejn-- and Jacob Zvi Zylbersztejn, my father. The pictures were taken after the war, in Israel, and sent to me. They both survived the camps. My mother was in Auschwitz and Bergen-Belsen. And my father survived Auschwitz and Dachau.

speaker: interviewer
text: About what year do you think the pictures were taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Where-- we got together in '45. After the war, they came back.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the pictures were taken when?

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- oh, after they went to Israel in '48. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] That's a picture of myself with my husband, taken in 1976 in Chicago. And the back of it says that it was a Thanksgiving dinner at a-- at a cousin's house.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name was David Lichtenstein. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Now this one is my daughter, Heddy, with her children-- Joshua, Davida, and Esther. And this was also taken about, I would say, three or four years ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken-- I'm not sure-- Chicago or Florida. I can't see it from this angle. This-- no. This must have been taken in Chicago. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is my daughter, Heddy, with her husband, Saul, her son, Josh, Davida. And Esther is the infant. I went to Chicago for her birth. So Esther is the little infant. She was born then.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken-- she's a July-- this must have been taken August-- about nine years ago. She was born in July. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And this is also my daughter with her two children, Davida and Esther. And I believe this was taken in Jamaica, in 1987. And her husband took it and enlarged it himself. I'm very fond of it.
